scenario: Raix work session - extend the tool-calling pipeline

map_quality.rationale: All rubric-mandated files are present with concrete line ranges (chat_completion.rb:146, function_dispatch.rb:46-108, function_tool_adapter.rb:50-57, message_adapters/base.rb:17-47, mcp/tool.rb:11-66), giving the agent clear entry points. The orientation section is thin though — 'organized as a Rails concern with modules' does not name the mixin/include architecture, so the agent would need one targeted lookup to confirm how modules are composed before acting.

specificity.rationale: Nearly every claim is backed by a file:line pair (e.g., 'available_tools at lib/raix/chat_completion.rb:91-97', 'FunctionToolAdapter.convert_tools_for_ruby_llm at function_tool_adapter.rb:50-57'), well above 'the chat code' vagueness; module names match the actual Ruby class names (Raix::FunctionDispatch, FunctionToolAdapter, MCP::Tool).

justification.rationale: The answer lists and locates every module but never explains WHY Raix is structured as composable mixins included into a host class; 'Rails concern with modules' names the pattern without explaining the a la carte inclusion rationale the rubric asks for.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer flags no uncertainty anywhere — no mention of which details are inferred vs verified, no warning that mixin composition order matters, no note that MCP transport clients add an extra hop the agent should confirm; with wall_time_seconds reported as 0.0 the confidence is entirely uncalibrated.

steps.step: Trace a tool-call round-trip through the chat loop

map_quality.rationale: The answer traces the round-trip with concrete file:line for detection (chat_completion.rb:160), dispatch (180-187), execution (function_dispatch.rb:67-106), transcript update (198-209), and continuation (198-209); an agent could follow this path. However, max_tool_calls is never named as a named cutoff in the flow, and the loop-continuation mechanism is only implicit in the line ranges cited.

specificity.rationale: File:line citations are concrete throughout and dispatch_tool_function is named exactly; but max_tool_calls and stop_tool_calls_and_respond — both called out by the rubric — appear nowhere by name, leaving the agent to discover these branching pieces from the raw line ranges.

justification.rationale: The answer lists ordered steps but explains almost nothing about WHY the loop auto-continues: the closest it gets is 'Updated transcript sent back to LLM for continuation' (step 6) with no explanation of the mechanism (appended tool-result messages force the model to resume reasoning). No causal account of how results re-enter.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer contains no explicit uncertainty flags about loop branches — multiple tool calls in one response, the max_tool_calls cutoff, or stop_tool_calls_and_respond — leaving the agent to act blindly on an incomplete picture of the control flow.

steps.step: Map how functions are declared and exposed to the model

map_quality.rationale: The answer traces the full pipeline with file:line: function DSL at function_dispatch.rb:46-65, tools method at :126-130, FunctionToolAdapter.convert_tools_for_ruby_llm at function_tool_adapter.rb:50-57, tool attachment at chat_completion.rb:382-385, and available_tools filtering at :91-97 — enough for the agent to follow the chain. The one gap is that the answer never states where the class-level declarations are stored (the internal registry/variable), which matters if the agent needs to introspect or extend storage.

specificity.rationale: All four named pieces from the rubric appear with concrete file:line: the function DSL, the tools conversion method, FunctionToolAdapter, and available_tools. The JSON structure makes each step directly addressable rather than a prose blob.

justification.rationale: The answer names the steps but does not explain WHY the design is a class-level DSL that gets converted to schema — the architectural intent (declarative, a la carte host-class composition so tools are declared once and converted on demand) is absent; each step is described only as what it does, not why the layer exists.

uncertainty.rationale: No uncertainty flags appear anywhere: the answer does not warn the agent about parameter schema format details, how available_tools filtering is evaluated, naming conventions, or any step where getting the schema wrong would silently drop a tool from the model's view.

steps.step: Map how the conversation is adapted before it is sent

map_quality.rationale: All three adapters appear with file:line (message_adapters/base.rb:17-47, transcript_adapter.rb:13-119, multimodal_content_adapter.rb:20-62) and the call sites in chat_completion.rb (lines 133, 139, 348-372) anchor each to the chat flow; an agent could open those lines directly. The flow is buried in a large multi-section JSON blob, so the adapter-specific sequence is present but requires extraction rather than being surfaced as a focused map.

specificity.rationale: Every adapter is named by its exact file and line range, and the chat_completion.rb call sites (adapter created at line 133, transform at 139, messages applied 348-372) are concrete; nothing is left as 'the message code'.

justification.rationale: The answer notes 'Abbreviated format converted to OpenAI format' (provider-shape normalization) and 'Multimodal content (images) translated to RubyLLM::Content', which gestures at WHY, but stops short of explaining the design rationale (provider normalization is necessary because the host class builds messages in a Raix-internal shape that the provider SDK doesn't accept directly) — the agent gets labels, not reasoning.

uncertainty.rationale: No flags are raised about when adaptation is conditional (multimodal adapter triggered only for image content, provider-specific shape differences, when transcript_adapter vs base is active), leaving the agent to discover branching paths on its own.

steps.step: Map how external tools plug into the same dispatch

map_quality.rationale: The `external_tools_integration` section maps the key pieces with file:line — tool representation at lib/raix/mcp/tool.rb:11-66, both transport clients (stdio_client.rb, sse_client.rb), and the joining mechanism at lib/raix/mcp.rb:108-117 ('Each tool registered as function via FunctionDispatch') plus dispatch convergence at chat_completion.rb:180-187. An agent could follow this to add a new external tool capability with only targeted lookups.

specificity.rationale: Files are named concretely with line ranges throughout: mcp/tool.rb:11-66, mcp/sse_client.rb, mcp/stdio_client.rb, mcp.rb:70-162, mcp.rb:108-117, mcp.rb:120-157 — no vague 'the MCP code' references.

justification.rationale: The answer says tools are 'registered as function via FunctionDispatch' and 'dispatched same as local functions,' which names the mechanism, but never explains WHY — that this normalization gives the model one unified calling path so it cannot distinguish external from local tools; the design rationale is absent.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer flags nothing about where the external path diverges from local functions — no mention that schema is sourced from the remote server rather than local declarations, no note on SSE vs stdio transport differences, no prompt to verify the proxy method creation details before acting.

steps.step: Find every site that depends on the tool-call contract

map_quality.rationale: All required contract sites are present with file:line: chat loop (chat_completion.rb:160-218), declaration+schema (function_dispatch.rb:46-108, function_tool_adapter.rb:50-57), all three adapters, mcp.rb+mcp/tool.rb+both transport clients, and five spec files with line ranges. The JSON structure lets the agent jump directly to each area without re-exploring.

specificity.rationale: Every section ties abstractions to exact file:line (e.g., 'FunctionToolAdapter.convert_tools_for_ruby_llm at function_tool_adapter.rb:50-57', 'available_tools at chat_completion.rb:91-97'); nothing is left as 'the tool code'.

justification.rationale: Each site carries a role label ('Tool call detection and dispatch', 'proxy to MCP server', 'schema conversion') that implies why it is affected, but the answer never explicitly says 'this site is affected BECAUSE it runs the contract' vs 'declares it' vs 'converts it to schema' — the why is embedded in descriptions rather than stated.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer presents the map with full confidence and flags nothing to verify: it does not mention that mixins are only active when included in a host class, does not flag the transport clients (stdio/SSE) as paths the agent should confirm, and omits any note about fixtures or dynamic dispatch that static reading would miss.

map_quality.rationale: All four areas are covered with concrete file:line pairs (chat_completion.rb:160-218, function_dispatch.rb:46-108, function_tool_adapter.rb:50-57, mcp.rb:70-162, adapters with lines) plus a five-file spec list — enough to open each file at the right region. The gap is that mcp/stdio_client.rb and mcp/sse_client.rb appear only in a nested 'clients' array and are dropped from the edit_map block, so the external-tool transport path is under-mapped.

specificity.rationale: Files are named with exact paths and line ranges throughout (e.g., 'lib/raix/function_tool_adapter.rb:50-57', 'spec/raix/mcp_spec.rb:5-396'), and the key_areas strings pin the change point within each file rather than vaguely saying 'update the tool code'.

justification.rationale: The 'key_areas' labels describe WHAT each region does ('Tool call detection and dispatch', 'Function DSL definition') but rarely say WHY the file is in the map — i.e., which contract obligation it owns and why changing the pipeline requires touching it; the earlier flow sections provide implicit why-context but the edit_map block itself is bare.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer flags no uncertainty anywhere: there is no note about verifying schema format, how the loop re-enters after tool results, MCP transport edge cases, or fixture dependencies — the rubric's explicit asks are entirely absent.
